title: Section 4

source.title: NPNF1-03. On the Holy Trinity; Doctrinal Treatises; Moral Treatises

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: If this faith be taken away from human affairs, who but must observe how great disorder in them, and how fearful confusion must follow?

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: For who will be loved by any with mutual affection, (being that the loving itself is invisible,) if what I see not, I ought not to believe?

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: Therefore will the whole of friendship perish, in that it consists not save of mutual love.

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: For what of it will it be able to receive from any, if nothing of it shall be believed to be shown?

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: Further, friendship perishing, there will be preserved in the mind the bonds neither of marriages, nor of kindreds and relations; because in these also there is assuredly a friendly union of sentiment.

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: Spouse therefore will not be able to love spouse in turn, inasmuch as each believes not the other’s love, because the love itself cannot be seen.

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: Nor will they long to have sons, who they believe not will make them a return.

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: And if these be born and grow up, much less will the parents themselves love their own children, whose love towards themselves in those children’s hearts they will not see, it being invisible; if it be not praiseworthy faith, but blameable rashness, to believe those things which are not seen.

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: Why should I now speak of the other connections, of brothers, sisters, sons-in-law, and fathers-in-law, and of them who are joined together by any kindred or affinity, if love is uncertain, and the will suspected, that of parents by sons, and that of sons by parents, whilst due benevolence is not rendered; because neither is it thought to be due, that which is not seen in another not being thought to exist.

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: Further, if this caution be not a mark of ability, but be hateful, wherein we believe not that we are loved, because we see not the love of them who love, and repay not them, unto whom we think not that we owe a return; to that degree are human affairs thrown into disorder, if what we see not we believe not, as to be altogether and utterly overthrown, if we believe no wills of men, which assuredly we cannot see.

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: I omit to mention in how many things they, who find fault with us because we believe what we see not, believe report or history; or concerning places where they have not themselves been; and say not, we believe not, because we have not seen.

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: Since if they say this, they are obliged to confess that their own parents are not surely known to them: because on this point also they have believed the accounts of others telling of it, who yet are unable to show it, because it is a thing already past; retaining themselves no sense of that time, and yet yielding assent without any doubting to others speaking of that time: and unless this be done, there must of necessity be incurred a faithless impiety towards parents, whilst we are, as it were, showing a rashness of belief in those things which we cannot see.

verses.book: Concerning Faith of Things Not Seen.
verses.text: Since therefore, if we believe not those things which we cannot see, human society itself, through concord perishing, will not stand how much more is faith to be applied to divine things, although they be not seen; failing the application of which, it is not the friendship of some men or other, but the very chiefest bond of piety that is violated, so as for the chiefest misery to follow.
